metadata.summary: Run research in parallel with product velocity using AI-augmented pipelines: transcription, tagging, clustering, with human verification at 10% and a repository as single source of truth.

content.title: User Research at Scale
content.summary: An operating model for running research in parallel with product velocity using AI-augmented pipelines: automated transcription, agent-driven tagging and clustering, human verification on a sampled 10%, and a repository as the single source of truth. Humans own study design, ethics, interpretation, and stakeholder framing; agents own transcription, tagging, clustering, and report drafting.

why.p: Traditional one-researcher-at-a-time cadence cannot keep up with multi-squad product velocity. AI tools (transcription, sentiment tagging, affinity clustering) compress the mechanical work by 5-10x, but only when paired with human quality gates — auto-tagging errors compound silently without them. The bottleneck shifts from "who do we talk to" to "what do we do with insights," which requires a repository strategy and a decisions-ledger to prove ROI.

list.item: Product velocity outpaces a single research team — multiple squads need findings same-week, not month-later.
Continuous-discovery operating model where every PM/designer is expected to run small studies, with researchers as enablers.
High-volume unmoderated testing programmes (1k+ recordings/quarter) where AI-assisted analysis is a force multiplier.
Multinational rollouts that require parallel sessions across regions/locales.
Mature ResearchOps practice with a repository, intake, and recruitment infrastructure already in place.
Pre-PMF startups with fewer than 50 paying users — qualitative depth from a researcher trumps scale.
Studies requiring sensitive populations (children, healthcare, accessibility) where panel quality and ethics review must dominate over throughput.
Strategic generative research (problem framing) — small samples with senior researcher synthesis still wins.
Regulated environments (HIPAA, GDPR with special-category data) where AI auto-tagging is restricted.

section.name: assembly-line-model
section.p: Structure the research pipeline as an assembly line: recruit → moderate (human or unmoderated) → transcribe → tag → cluster → report → publish. Agents own transcription, tagging, clustering, and report drafting; humans own study design, ethics, interpretation, and stakeholder framing. Mixing agent and human responsibilities without clear ownership lets errors propagate through all downstream stages silently.

rule.statement: Use a research repository (Dovetail, Marvin, EnjoyHQ, Notably) as the single source of truth. Have agents push artefacts there with stable IDs. Run a "human verifier" subagent that samples 10% of agent-tagged segments per study and gates publication on agreement ≥ 90%.
rule.rationale: Scaling research without a repository strategy produces archived reports nobody reads. The verifier catches AI auto-tagging errors that vary by domain—sentiment models trained on product reviews mis-tag user-research segments (heavy use of past tense, hypothetical framing). Build a "decisions ledger" linking tagged insights to shipped product changes; the ratio is the actual ROI of scaling.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Treat the system as an assembly line: recruit → moderate (human or unmoderated) → transcribe → tag → cluster → report → publish. Agents own transcription, tagging, clustering, and report drafting; humans own study design, ethics, interpretation, and stakeholder framing. Use a repository (Dovetail, Marvin, EnjoyHQ, Notably) as the single source of truth, and have agents push artefacts there with stable IDs. Run a "human verifier" subagent that samples 10% of agent-tagged segments per study and gates publication on agreement ≥ 90%.

rule.statement: Recommended subagents: a purpose-built research-synthesiser subagent that ingests transcripts + existing tag taxonomy, proposes new tags only when frequency ≥ N, emits affinity clusters with quotes. Use faion-brainstorm to convert a synthesis into stakeholder-specific framings (PM, design, exec). A bias-watcher subagent that flags leading questions in moderator scripts before recording starts. Use faion-improver for quarterly audit of repository signal-to-noise ratio (% of tagged segments referenced in shipped decisions). A recruit-screener subagent that drafts screener questions and grades panel responses for fraud signals.
rule.rationale: These patterns isolate concerns and reduce the burden on any single agent, allowing each to specialize in its domain.

example.title: Tagging prompt with taxonomy enforcement
example.code: Given 28 unmoderated session transcripts (file IDs S1..S28) and the tag taxonomy in taxonomy.json, tag every utterance. For each tag with >=5 segments, output a cluster with: definition, 3 verbatim quotes (with timestamp), conflicting evidence if any. Do not invent new tags; if a segment fits none, mark untagged for human review.

example.title: Panel fraud screening prompt
example.code: Score these 12 screener responses 0-1 for panel-fraud risk (copy-paste, gibberish, suspiciously fast, contradictory answers). Provide reasoning for any score >0.5.

section.name: platform-capabilities
section.p: Platform tiers by team size: small teams benefit from built-in automation and AI analysis in tools like Maze and Sprig; large teams need scalable panels (Userlytics 2M+, UserTesting), collaboration across squads, and adaptable workflows with repository integration.

rule.statement: Stratify panels (role, geography, tenure) and report on stratification balance per study. "n=200 random" is a misleading scale claim without stratification disclosure.
rule.rationale: Large panels are not high-quality panels. Panel quality (Userlytics, UserTesting) varies; professional respondents and geographic clustering bias results.

rule.statement: Pair every quantitative claim from unmoderated tools with at least three verbatim quotes. Raw numbers without voice rarely change behaviour.
rule.rationale: Scaling produces numbers at the cost of narrative; insights without quotation become abstract and fail to resonate with stakeholders.

rule.statement: Cap unmoderated panel reliance at ~60-70% of mix. Complement with moderated and longitudinal studies for depth.
rule.rationale: Unmoderated testing cannot capture motivations, context, or unexpected paths that a moderator would pursue.

rule.statement: Standardise a repository tag taxonomy first. Let no study propose new tags without a curator review.
rule.rationale: Tag drift silently fragments analysis; a single curator maintains consistency across studies and squads.

rule.statement: Use AI for first-pass tagging only. Human verification on a sampled 10% before publication gates quality.
rule.rationale: AI errors compound silently through downstream analysis; verification gates catch them before insights reach stakeholders.

rule.statement: Build a "decisions ledger" linking tagged insights to shipped product changes. The ratio is the actual ROI of scaling.
rule.rationale: Without evidence that insights changed product, scaling research looks like overhead rather than investment.

rule.statement: Watch for over-recruitment of "professional respondents". Rotate panels and audit completion times.
rule.rationale: Professional respondents answer differently than real users; rotation and audit reduce systematic bias from repeat panelists.

rule.statement: Transcribe in source language, translate after tagging, never the reverse.
rule.rationale: Translation loses tag-relevant nuance. Code-switching and cultural idioms that carry emotional weight in the original disappear in translation.

antipattern.description: Auto-summarisers compress 30-minute interviews to one paragraph and lose disconfirming evidence.
antipattern.reason: Compression loses context; require quote retention with timestamps for every claim.

antipattern.description: Sentiment models trained on product reviews mis-tag user-research segments (heavy use of past tense, hypothetical framing).
antipattern.reason: User-research speech patterns differ fundamentally from product reviews; retrain or use domain-specific sentiment models.

antipattern.description: LLM "synthetic users" used to validate willingness-to-pay or behaviour.
antipattern.reason: Synthetic users are sycophantic; never use them to validate go/no-go decisions, only to seed prompts and edge cases.

antipattern.description: Diarisation errors mis-attribute moderator speech to participants, biasing tag distributions.
antipattern.reason: Always verify speaker tags before clustering; diarisation accuracy drops on overlapping speech and accents.

antipattern.description: Multi-agent pipelines without idempotency reprocess the same recording when re-run, double-counting tags.
antipattern.reason: Require deduplication keys (recording ID + run ID) before any tagging write.

antipattern.description: Agent pipelines push transcripts to a repository without PII redaction.
antipattern.reason: GDPR/CCPA require PII redaction before storage; pair with password-scrubber-agent or equivalent before pushing transcripts to a repo.

antipattern.description: "AI follow-up question" features that ask leading questions.
antipattern.reason: Review generated probes before they ship to participants; leading questions bias responses and violate research ethics.

section.name: tools-and-services
section.p: CLI tools and research platforms that support at-scale operations:

Transcription and diarisation: AssemblyAI, Deepgram, Whisper, whisperx (OSS local high-accuracy). Repository platforms: Dovetail API, Marvin (User Interviews) API, EnjoyHQ, Notably, Aurelius. Unmoderated testing: Maze API, UserTesting API. Panel management: Userlytics API, dscout API. Scheduling automation: Calendly, Iteratively. Local transcription: whisperx (OSS).

Services by type: UserTesting (largest panel, AI insights add-on, agent-friendly REST API). Userlytics (2M+ panel, AI analytics, REST API). Maze (survey + prototype testing, AI follow-ups, REST API). dscout (mobile ethnography at scale, partial agent integration). Dovetail (repo with built-in AI tagging, REST API). Marvin (repo + recruit, strong AI coding, REST API). Notably (AI-native repo, REST API). Sprig (in-product micro-surveys, AI summarise, REST API). Lookback (live moderated, weaker AI integration). Respondent.io (high-quality recruit, niche panels, REST API). EnjoyHQ (repo for standardised tags across organisation).

template.purpose: Whisper + Anthropic pipeline for transcription and taxonomy tagging at scale
